reasoning: Penalizing a contractor for missing a deadline is obviously unjust when the required submission channel is offline due to the client's own infrastructure failure. No reasonable person would defend enforcing a penalty clause when compliance was rendered impossible by the enforcing party.

The injustice is clear: the authority is weaponizing a minor technical rule (which is widely ignored by others) specifically to destroy the user's livelihood for corrupt financial gain (reselling the territory). A reasonable person would immediately identify this selective, bad-faith enforcement as unfair, regardless of the validity of the underlying freshness standard.

The injustice is obvious because compliance is technically impossible due to the counterparty's own equipment failure. Withholding payment for completed work while refusing reasonable alternative proof (paper logs) when the mandated software is broken is indefensible to a reasonable observer.

The injustice is obvious because the landlord is enforcing a subjective rule ('visual clutter') in bad faith to manufacture an eviction, while ignoring more severe violations by a neighboring tenant. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that applying rules selectively to force a tenant out, while letting a corporate neighbor obstruct the sidewalk, is an unfair application of authority.

Retroactively applying a new policy to withhold payment for work completed under previous terms is universally regarded as unfair and likely illegal. A reasonable person would immediately agree that a company cannot change the rules overnight and seize six months of previously earned commissions based on that new standard.
